In the Time Before Time, the world was a canvas of endless gray. There was no sound but the sigh of the wind, and no color but the pale mist. The Great Weaver, solitary and bored, plucked a single thread of silver from her hair and strung it across the sky.

"Let there be song," she whispered. She plucked the silver thread, and a low hum vibrated through the valleys. But the sound was lonely.

So, the Weaver reached into the heart of the earth and pulled out a thread of vibrant green—the first vine. She strung it next to the silver. When she plucked them together, the sound was a harmony, and the first trees burst from the soil, dancing to the rhythm. Encouraged, she pulled a thread of blue from the deep ocean and a thread of gold from the heart of a sleeping volcano.

With every new string she added to her celestial harp, the world filled with color. The crimson of the sunset, the indigo of the deep night, the yellow of the finch. Finally, she strummed all the strings at once. The resulting chord was so powerful it shattered the silence forever, birthing the first thunderstorm, whose crashing drums still remind us of the Weaver’s first great song.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, before the sky had eyes, the Night was a thick, heavy blanket of obsidian velvet that smothered the world in silence. The people lived in fear of the dark, huddling around dying embers, praying for the return of the Sun, who was a fickle traveler.

One evening, a young weaver named Elara grew tired of the fear. She took her needle, forged from the silver of a fallen moonbeam, and climbed the highest peak, the Whispering Spire. At the summit, the darkness was absolute. Elara did not tremble. Instead, she began to stitch. She pricked the fabric of the heavy Night, pulling her silver thread through.

With every puncture, a drop of the universe’s ancient light bled through the hole. She stitched patterns of hunters, bears, and flowing rivers, creating maps for travelers and stories for children. Her fingers bled, but the sky brightened.

Seeing her bravery, the Sun, returning from his journey, kissed the horizon. But he did not banish her work. Instead, he allowed the Night to remain, now a beautiful tapestry of a billion pinpricks. The people looked up and no longer saw a smothering blanket, but a ceiling of diamonds, realizing that even in the deepest dark, the light is always waiting to bleed through.
